coredata.dc:title: Striking luminescence phenomena of carbon dots and their applications as a double ratiometric fluorescence probes for H2S detection

Chen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Here, we report some new observations on the luminescence of carbon dots (CDs) that are passivated with polyethylene glycol, and explored them, for the first time, as highly sensitive H2S detection probe. The as-prepared CDs have an ultraviolet (UV) emission at 350 nm and a green emission at 540 nm when excited at 270 nm, but they have only a green emission at 523 nm when excited at 365 nm. As Na2S is added to the CDs, the UV emission decreases gradually, while the green emission increases slightly, and interestingly a new emission appears at 455 nm is increased linearly in intensity with the increase of Na2S concentration. The blue emission at 455 nm is from the intrinsic core of CDs, and the green emissions at 540 or 523 nm are from their surface states, while the UV emission at 350 nm is from the anchored PEG. Both the emission intensity ratios of F450/F350 and F455/F523 show an excellent linear relationship with the Na2S concentration in the range of 0–800 μM, and the detection limit estimated from both of them is approximately 7.0 nM, indicating our method is the most sensitive H2S CDs based detection method reported thus far. Furthermore, the detection was tested successfully for H2S imaging in live cells. We propose that the interactions of H2S with the CDs and the anchored ligands, as well as the energy transfer between the ligands and the CDs, are responsible for the luminescence responses for H2S detection. Our studies enrich the world of carbon dots with plenty of potential applications.

head.ce:title: Striking luminescence phenomena of carbon dots and their applications as a double ratiometric fluorescence probes for H2S detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we report some new observations on the luminescence of carbon dots (CDs) that are passivated with polyethylene glycol, and explored them, for the first time, as highly sensitive H2S detection probe. The as-prepared CDs have an ultraviolet (UV) emission at 350 nm and a green emission at 540 nm when excited at 270 nm, but they have only a green emission at 523 nm when excited at 365 nm. As Na2S is added to the CDs, the UV emission decreases gradually, while the green emission increases slightly, and interestingly a new emission appears at 455 nm is increased linearly in intensity with the increase of Na2S concentration. The blue emission at 455 nm is from the intrinsic core of CDs, and the green emissions at 540 or 523 nm are from their surface states, while the UV emission at 350 nm is from the anchored PEG. Both the emission intensity ratios of F450/F350 and F455/F523 show an excellent linear relationship with the Na2S concentration in the range of 0–800 μM, and the detection limit estimated from both of them is approximately 7.0 nM, indicating our method is the most sensitive H2S CDs based detection method reported thus far. Furthermore, the detection was tested successfully for H2S imaging in live cells. We propose that the interactions of H2S with the CDs and the anchored ligands, as well as the energy transfer between the ligands and the CDs, are responsible for the luminescence responses for H2S detection. Our studies enrich the world of carbon dots with plenty of potential applications.

The schematic diagram of synthesis and application of CDs for H2S detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Yang Xu and Lalit Chudal, Methodology, Synthesis and Characterization; Hongmei Yu and Wei Chen, Conceptualization, Instruction and Writing; Shuping Deng, Xiuhui Zhu, Shaoyan Wang, Funding and Mentoring; Nil Kanatha Pandey, Eric Horacio Amador, Brian Bui, Data analysis and editing; Lingyun Wang and Xuedan Ma, Result evaluation and writing.

Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) has been known for years to be a toxic and flammable gas with a distinct rotten-egg odor. It is heavier than air and when burned, it produces highly toxic vapors, such as sulfur dioxide. Exposure to H2S can cause many severe reactions, such as severe eye and respiratory irritations, headache, nausea, fluid in the lungs, shock, convulsions, coma, and even death [1]. Despite its toxicity, in the past two decades, H2S has been recognized as a gaseous signal molecule [2,3]. It is a biologically active gas termed as a gasotransmitter or gasomediator [4–6]. Recent studies showed that H2S is of paramount importance for cellular functions in addition to operating as a neuromodulator and neuroprotectant [2,3]. Along with carbon monoxide (CO) and nitric oide (NO), H2S is one of the three endogenous gasotransmitters which are critically important for life [5–8].

The function of H2S as a neuromodulator and neuroprotectant occurs by increasing reduced glutathione levels, which subsequently protect neurons from death [4]. Furthermore, H2S was discovered to open KATP and Cl− channels in these cells, thereby contributing to its protective effects [9]. Another function of H2S is that it acts as a vasodilator in mammalian blood vessels. Additionally, it protects the heart, kidneys, and other organs from damages after ischemia or reperfusion injury. H2S also has therapeutic effects in the central nervous system (CNS) diseases. In vitro and in vivo experiments suggested that H2S plays a protective role in the progression of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) [2,6,7]. Further investigations revealed the similar roles of H2S on some CNS disorders with reduced dopamine levels, including Bipolar disorder (BD) and Parkinson’s Disease (PD) [3,9]. Likewise, H2S abnormalities were found to correlate with diabetes and heart disease [7]. Interestingly, new studies suggest that H2S supplementation may provide a new solution to overcome diseases related to oxygen deficiency. For example, it has been reported that H2S pre-treated mice survived much longer than control mice in hypoxic conditions (5% oxygen) [10]. These exciting observations may indicate a new way to overcome oxygen-related diseases, such as cancer, diabetes, and heart-related diseases [5–7,9]. Based on these observations, the detection and monitor of H2S are crucial not only for health but also for security and safety.

Several methods have been developed and tested for H2S monitoring [11–15], however, it should be noted that H2S detection in biological systems is very complicated as H2S is affected by many parameters [16–19]. Currently, the most common method for detecting and measuring H2S is by trapping it with zinc, followed by acidification and allowing it to react with N-dimethyl-p-phenylenediamine [13]. Consequently, the resulting product, methylene blue, can be measured spectrophotometrically. The limitation of using this method is that the final product is interfered by other colored substances. The ion-sensitive electrode method has also been reported for H2S detection, but its selectivity and sensitivity have some issues [20]. Measurement of H2S in vivo and in vitro has been tested by the monobromobimane method. A fluorescence probe has been described using the thiolysis of dinitrophenyl ether moiety, which is effective at circumneutral pH and exhibits a large fluorescence enhancement with distinctive color changes [21]. It can detect H2S as low as 90 nM [21]. Recently, gold nanoparticles have been used to detect H2S and this method has some advantages as it can be used for in vitro and in vivo detection or imaging [22]. A nitro olefin functionalized probe was fabricated as a selective fluorescent probe for detecting H2S with a very high detection sensitivity [23]. Some fluorescent probes for H2S detection have been proposed based on (1) the reduction of (p-nitrosodimethylaniline) RNO2 or (azide) RN3 to the parent amine moieties, (2) nucleophilic reactions, (3) sulfide-selective coordination with Cu2+ complexes, (4) sulfide-selective cleavage reaction [23–25]. Nevertheless, these detection systems always contain organic solvents due to the poor hydrophilicity of dyes, which greatly limits their biological application. Most recently, photoacoustic imaging has been combined with optics [26,27] or magnetic imaging [28] for H2S detection in vivo. The advantage of photoacoustic probing is increased imaging penetration. However, the photoacoustic method is not as sensitive as fluorescence detection.

Fluorescent CDs are good candidates for biosensing or imaging because of their good biocompatibility, aqueous dispersibility, and excellent cell permeability [29]. Wu et al. found CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for monitoring H2S in cell-free media and living cells [14], where CDs and naphthalimide-azide serve as the energy donor and energy acceptor, respectively. However, this method is time-consuming and tedious because naphthalimide-azide is synthesized by three-step reactions and finally is chemically linked to CDs. A new detection method based on a CD serving as both the fluorophore and the anchoring site for the ligands, which can attach with Cu2+ ions, has been reported [30]. In this system, Cu2+ ions efficiently quench the CDs’ fluorescence. However, when S2− ions are introduced into the system, fluorescence enhancement occurs, resulting in a turn-on sensor for S2− ions with a detection limit of 0.78 μM [30]. In a similar fashion, a two metal-ion-mediated fluorescent carbon dots for indirect detection of sulfide ions. In the CD system, CD/Hg2+ and CD/Ag+ were used as a fluorescence enhancement and a fluorescence quenching probes for sulfur ions detection, respectively [31]. The two probes allowed detection of sulfide over the range of 2–10 μM and 1–100 μM, and the detection limit of 0.32 μM and 0.43 μM, respectively. One drawback of these methods is the formation of heavy metal sulfides. These heavy metals are highly toxic and are harmful to biomedical applications. Meanwhile, there is still a large room for improvement in terms of the detection window and limit of detection. Most H2S fluorescent probes can provide only a single fluorescence signal, which is not very reliable in many cases. Therefore, easily accessible CDs with high sensitivity and selectivity are highly desirable for H2S detection.

Herein, we propose a double ratiometric fluorescence probe for H2S monitoring based on our observation of the luminescence of CDs that are passivated with PEG. The as-synthesized samples have a UV (350 nm) and green emissions (540 nm) when excited at 270 nm, but only have a green emission (523 nm) when excited at 365 nm. After adding Na2S to the CDs, the UV emission decreases, while the green emission increases, and more interestingly a new emission appears at 455 nm, which increases linearly while increasing the concentration of Na2S. The blue emission (455 nm) is from the intrinsic core of the CDs and the green emission (540 or 523 nm) is from their surface states, while the UV emission (350 nm) is from the anchored PEG. Both the intensity ratios (F450/F350 and F455/F523) show excellent linearity in a wide range of the Na2S concentration (0–800 μM) and the limit of detection estimated from both of them is approximately 7.0 nM, demonstrating our proposed method is the most sensitive H2S detection method reported so far. The double ratio fluorescent probe reported here was constructed directly from a single carbon point for the detection of H2S, and the method is much more straightforward and simpler.

Para-phenylenediamine, Polyethylene glycol (PEG) 200, anhydrous ethanol, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, NaBr, Na2S, CH3COONa, NaHSO4, NaNO2, Na3PO4, NaHCO3, NaHSO3, Na2SO3, NaF, NaOH, KSCN, and NaCl were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. NaH2PO4 was provided by Tianjin KeMiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were used as obtained without further purification. The resistivity ≥18.2 MΏ ultra-pure water was used throughout the experiment.

0.216 g of para-phenylenediamine, 5 mL of PEG 200, and 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol were mixed into a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor, which was placed in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h to form CDs. Then, the mixtures in the reactor were cooled to room temperature and filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane in order to get rid of the impurities. The resulting product was dialyzed against water for 48 h in the air at room temperature, and then the CDs solution was condensed by rotary evaporation and further lyophilized to obtain pure CD powder samples.

An EM420 transmission electron microscope (Philips, Holland) was used to determine the sizes and morphologies of the CDs. The X-ray diffraction pattern (PANalytical, Holland) was measured at the 2θ range from 3° to 90°. A Lambda 900 UV–visible light spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) was used to measure the optical properties in water between 200 and 800 nm (UV-VIS) wavelength. A LS55 Luminescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) was used to measure the fluorescence spectrum. A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was employed to determine surface functional groups of the CDs. The ESCALAB 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was utilized to measure the chemical compositions and the chemical energy structures of CDs. The Raman spectra were collected on a BX41 (Olympus, Japan). The lifetime was measured utilizing the FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, France). The Absolute quantum yield was performed with the C11347 Quantaurus QY Instrument (Hamamatsu Photonics, Japan).

A volume of 3 mL of CDs (0.3 mg/mL, pH = 5.0 PBS solution) was added into a quartz colorimetric utensil followed by the addition of various concentrations of Na2S. The reaction mixture was allowed to react at ambient temperature under the dark condition for 7s. Subsequently, photoluminescence spectra were collected with the excitation wavelengths of 270 nm or 365 nm. The calibration curves were established with F450/F350 or F455/F523 ratio as a function of H2S concentration.

To verify the practicality of the sensor for detecting H2S in different water samples, lake water and tap water were used. The lake water was taken from the Yingze Lake (University of Science and Technology Liaoning, China). The tap water was drawn from our lab (Applied Chemistry Laboratory). The two samples were filtered using a 0.22 μm filter and then stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was performed to determine the cytotoxicity of CDs. MCF-7 cell line was chosen for this purpose. CDs stock solution (10 mg/mL) was dispersed in ethanol by sonication for 5 min. The stock solution was diluted in cell media directly to obtain desired concentrations (1.5–200 μg/mL). 10,000 cells/well were seeded into a 96 well plate and then incubated for 24 h to allow the completion of cell attachment. After that, the culture media were removed, and all MCF-7 cells were washed three times with PBS buffer and were then treated with fresh media containing CDs (0–200 μg/mL) for 12 h. After removing media, the cells were incubated with media containing 0.5 mg/mL of MTT solution for another 3 h to facilitate the formation of formazan crystals. The experiments were performed three times independently.

The cell viability was determined using the following equation:
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
=
Absorbance of treated group Absorbance of control group
∗
100 %

To evaluate whether CDs can detect the H2S in cell environment, 200,000 cells were seeded in a glass-bottom imaging plate and incubated for 24 h in order to allow the completion of cell attachment. 1 mL of CDs solution (50 μg/mL) was injected into each imaging plates and incubated for 3 h. After removing cell media, the cells were washed with PBS (1x) for 3 times to remove free CDs. The cells were then incubated with fresh media containing Na2S (0, 100, 250, and 500 μM) for 1 h. The cells were then washed with PBS (1x) and then imaged with Olympus IX71 (Japan). Ex/Em 350/457 and 350/523 nm were used to image blue and green luminescence of CDs, respectively. All images were captured under the same experimental conditions (identical exposure time and sensitivity). The intensity was quantified using Image J software as follows: first, the background was subtracted and then thresholded so that only CDs fluorescence was selected. Finally, the average fluorescence intensity was measured in terms of gray value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size, morphology, elemental, and chemical composition of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images show that CDs are mainly spherical, with an average size of about 0.93 nm (Fig. 1 a–c). As shown in Fig. 1d, their sizes are in the range of 0.55–1.35 nm with an acceptable normal distribution. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 1e shows the steamed roll-shaped peak at the position of 2θ = 21°, which is equivalent to the characteristics of amorphous carbon. This indicates that the CDs have an amorphous structure with an sp3 hybridization core as described in the literature [32–34]. To further investigate the surface of the CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used. The XPS peaks at 285, 400, 532 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s are observed, respectively (Fig. 1f). The C1s chemical band (Fig. 1g) can be deconvoluted into three peaks, which correspond to the binding energies of sp2 carbons (CC, 284.7 eV), sp3 carbons (C–O/C–N, 286.0 eV), and carbonyl carbon (CO, 288.1eV), respectively [33,35]. The O1s chemical band can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.5, 532.31, and 533.0 eV, which originate from CO, C–O–C/C–OH, and C–O, respectively (Fig. 1h). Similarly, the N1s chemical band is composed of three peaks at 398.5, 399.4, and 400.1eV, which correspond to pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. 1i).

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the yellowish CDs solution shows green emission under 365 nm light. It can be seen that the CDs have multiple absorption peaks (Fig. 2b), which are from the carbonic cores and surface states, respectively. The first peak at 282 nm corresponds to the π-π∗ transition from the sp2 hybridization in the carbon cores, while a weak absorption at about 355 nm is ascribed to the n-π∗ transition of CO at the surface states [32,36–42]. The broad absorption from 400 to 600 nm may be from the π−π∗ aromatic fluorophore structure [43].

Fig. 2c displays the PL spectra of CDs with various excitation wavelengths at 258, 270, 324, 338, and 378 nm, respectively. The PL spectra of CDs with an excitation wavelength of 338 nm shows the strongest peak at 450 nm. This is in agreement with the reported data that isolated sp2 clusters within the carbon-oxygen sp3 matrix have blue emission because of the localization of electron-hole pairs [43]. Also, CDs show an excitation-dependent emission behavior. CDs show PL at a multi-peak centered at 350 nm, and also two other emission bands at 450 and 540 nm when excited at 258 nm.

We observed a blue emission at 450 nm that was present on excitation at 338 nm, and a blue-green emission at 480 nm was present on excitation at 378 nm. Two emission bands at 450 and 540 nm were found on excitation at 270 and 324 nm. Similarly, the excitation spectra are dependent on the emission wavelength. When the emission is centered at 363 nm, the main excitation peak is at 300 nm with two minuscule shoulders at 260 and 325 nm. For the emission wavelength peak at 450 nm, two main excitation peaks are observed at 260 and 330 nm with a shoulder at 380 nm. Furthermore, when the emission is at 530 nm, three main excitation peaks are observed at 275, 340, and 390 nm with two tiny shoulders at 425 and 450 nm, respectively. These phenomena indicate that at least three luminescence centers are responsible for the luminescence from the CDs, which we will discuss in the mechanism session. The quantum yield of CDs is approximately 10% on excitation at 365 nm, which is close to other reported CDs [35,44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2S by ratiometric luminescence method
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of CDs to detect H2S in aqueous solution was investigated using Na2S as a source of H2S. CDs showed an emission peak at 523 nm with excitation at 365 nm. Interestingly, after adding Na2S solution, the PL emission at 523 nm increased slightly, while a new emission at 455 nm strengthened quickly (Fig. 3 a). The F455/F523 ratio enhanced linearly with the increase of Na2S concentration (Fig. 3b). The excellent linearity with Na2S concentration in the range of 0–800 μM with the calibration equation as F455/F523 = 0.00166C + 0.43486 and R2 = 0.9990 was obtained, where C is Na2S concentration in μM (Fig. 3). Using the 3σ rule [46,47], the detection limit is calculated to be 7.0 nM. Furthermore, F455/F523 remained constant even after a prolonged time, indicating the viable signal stability of CDs for H2S detection (Fig. 3c).

Interestingly, the CDs also exhibited ratiometric response in the presence of Na2S upon excitation at 270 nm. As displayed in Fig. 4 a, CDs showed two emission peaks at 350 and 550 nm. After adding Na2S, the emission at 350 nm quenched, whereas the emission at 550 nm blue-shifted with enhanced intensity while a new emission at 450 nm appeared. The ratio of F450/F350 shows excellent linearity in the Na2S concentration range of 0–800 μM with a linear regression equation as: F450/F350 = 0.00208C + 0.0236 with linearity of R2 = 0.9954 and limit of detection (LOD) = 6.9 nM (Fig. 4b). The LOD is comparable or much lower than most probes, as summarized in Table 1 . Moreover, the linearity range (0–800 μM) is much wider than previously reported sensors. Two different ratiometric signals can be easily achieved through tuning excitation wavelengths.

Response time can provide information about the sensitivity of the probe. The time-dependent fluorescence intensity at 455 nm of CDs showed that this value could remain unchanged after 7 s upon the addition of 500 μM of Na2S. If more Na2S were added, less than 10 s was needed to reach the maximum fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5 a). The reaction kinetic curves further confirm these quick response characters between CDs and different concentrations of Na2S (Fig. 5b). The short response time indicates that CDs are suitable for real-time H2S detection.

Encouraged by the distinct ratiometric response of CDs upon treatment with Na2S, we further evaluated the detection selectivity by co-incubating with various biologically relevant anions and amino acids. Fig. 6 a shows that only Na2S induced obvious F455/F523 ratio enhancement, while negligible changes of F455/F523 were observed upon the addition of other anions species, such as Ac−, Br−, CO3 2−, F−, HCO3 −, HSO3 −, HSO4 −, NO2 −, PO4 3−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, Cl−, SCN−, H2PO4 −, and OH−. Similarly, CDs are not sensitive to amino acid (Fig. 6b). In addition, to test whether the CDs could detect H2S selectively even in the presence of other species, competitive anion experiments were performed. Our findings demonstrated that different competitive species did not result in any noticeable change in F455/F523, and H2S still resulted in a similar fluorescence change in the presence of competitive anions (Fig. 6c). These results indicated that CDs allowed selectivity and sensitivity of CDs for H2S detection by fluorometric analyses. So, we can rule out the interference of typical ions in the practical applications of CDs in cell and cell-free systems.

To evaluate the feasibility of practical application of H2S detection, CDs-based ratiometric luminescence method was carried out in lake water and tap water, respectively. Various amounts of H2S were mixed into the water samples, then F455/F523 of CDs was used to determine the H2S concentration based on the linear plot (Fig. 3b). As can be seen in Table 2 , the satisfactory H2S recoveries of 96.5%–105.0% with an RSD (relative standard deviation) lower than 4.9% was obtained in both the lake and the tap water samples. These results showed that CDs are a promising candidate for practical applications in H2S detection with ratiometric fluorescence signal.

CDs exhibit a negligible cytotoxicity at concentrations up to 200 μg/mL with 80% viability of MCF-7 cells after 24 h incubation using MTT assay (Fig. 7 a), which means CDs have an acceptable biocompatibility. The low cytotoxicity of CDs is beneficial to H2S detection in vitro and in vivo. Additionally, we chose 50 μg/mL of CDs as an optimum dose for all cell imaging experiments.

We investigated the imaging performance of CDs in MCF-7 breast cancer cells at different Na2S concentrations (Fig. 7b). It could be observed that CDs emitted strong green fluorescence and weak blue fluorescence, where the ratio (Iblue/Igreen) is 0.25. After incubating with Na2S (250 μM), the emission in the blue channel clearly strengthened, but the green emission had no obvious change. The blue emission channel enhanced with increased Na2S concentration. The ratio (Iblue/Igreen) increased from ∼0.25 to ∼0.75 as Na2S concentration increased (Fig. 7c). These results indicate that CDs can be used for detecting Na2S within living cells.

Understanding the structure and their luminescence properties are the key to uncover the mechanism for H2S detection. As formed CDs were anchored by PEG, and the structure comprised of sp2/sp3 carbon and oxygen/nitrogen-based groups as schematically shown in Fig. 8 . However, the actual mechanism of CD fluorescence is not completely understood yet. Among various mechanisms, three possible mechanisms have been proposed for carbon dot luminescence [59]: 1) the quantum confinement effect (QCE) or conjugated π-domains; for some CDs having large conjugated π-domains and few functional groups on the surface, QCE of conjugated π electrons and bandgap of conjugated π-domains are responsible for fluorescence of CDs [37,38,60]. For the larger size of CDs conjugated π-domain, the redshift of the emission peak has been found due to the smaller bandgap. The conjugated π-domain with confined size results in the bandgap, and the recombination of holes and electrons leads to bandgap fluorescence. The optical properties of CDs are dictated by the energy state of π-electron of the sp2 carbon cluster that is dispersed in the sp3 carbon skeleton [37,42,45,60,61]. The size and hence bandgap of CDs can be adjusted to tune their fluorescence. 2) the surface state, which is originated from the hybridization of the carbon backbone and the attached chemical groups; The surface defect is a boundary region or a spherical shell, which is different from the carbon core. The surface comprised of different chemical groups (sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons), or other functional groups, or dangling bonds [37,44,45,60,61]. Surface defects primarily originate from the oxidation of the surface and act as a capture center for excitons, leading to surface defect state fluorescence. Among various functional groups, some are fluorescence activation states, while others are silent state under a certain condition. Functional groups, depending upon their ability to supply electrons, have distinct energy states resulting in a series of emission traps [62,63]. Overall, CDs’ emission wavelength can be tuned by changing surface functional groups or changing the degree of oxidation of functional groups. 3) the molecule state, which is determined by the fluorescent molecules attached to the surface or interior of the carbon backbone. These small molecules attached to the surface or interior of the CDs exhibit fluorescence emission directly [62]. Although molecular fluorescence has higher QYs than bandgap fluorescence originated from the conjugated π-domains, their luminescence stability is limited. Taken together, the interactions among the bandgap fluorescence, the molecular fluorescence, and the surface state emission help to elucidate the fluorescence mechanism and have a great role in the color tuning of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence characteristics and origins of current carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: We noticed that most reported CDs exhibit only one or two emission peaks in the visible range. However, our CDs have three strikingly different emission peaks in the UV (350 nm), blue (455 nm), and green (540 nm) as shown in Fig. 9 . First, CDs did not show the blue emission at 455 nm when excited at 270 nm or 365 nm, while this peak appeared when Na2S was added, and it increased gradually with the concentration of Na2S. Second, the changes in the three emissions with Na2S concentration are very different – the UV emission decreases while the blue emission increases; however, the green emission first increases and then decreases. Third, the excitation spectra for the emissions at 350, 455, and 540 nm are obviously different, as shown in Table 3 and Fig. 9. All these indicate that the luminescence centers for the three emissions are different, and the interaction of Na2S with these three components or luminescence centers determine their changes, which provide a method for H2S detection.

The UV emission: To the best of our knowledge, only a few publications [64–68] reported the UV emissions from CDs, and only two papers [66,67] described three emissions from CDs that involves the UV emissions. Researchers disagree on the mechanisms of the UV emission of CDs. It was speculated that this emission to an unsaturated organic compound produced during the synthesis process as a by-product [67]. The deep UV emission of the self-passivated GQDs was assigned to the π electron transition of CC in the core of carbon dots [64]. They also pointed out that the self-passivated layer on the GQDs surface plays a vital role in the observed deep UV emission. Their carbon dots comprised of CC core and O and H containing surface functional groups (C–H, C–OH, CO, C–O–C). These groups on the surface of the CDs create “surface states” which are the energy levels between π and π∗ states of CC [64]. Zhu et al. reported three emissions with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Based on the time-resolved emission spectra, they believe that the UV emission (350–370 nm) resulted from the bandgap state associated with carbon core and the size of carbon core can efficiently adjust the bandgap of conjugate π-domains [66]. The blue emission (420 nm) was attributed to surface defects due to the oxidation of the surface, while the green emission (520 nm) was attributed to the aggregated molecular state [66].Various blue light-emitting CDs synthesized by a microwave method has been reported [68]. They used different amino acids to control the amount of nitrogen and carbon and related chemical bonds to tune the emission from different trapping states in the band structure of CDs. They observed that the amino groups are vital in the emission spectra (peak at 445 nm), and the fingerprint emissions corresponding to CO groups, N-containing heterocycles (pyridine N), C–N (graphitic N), and C–C/CC (intrinsic C) are found at about 500 nm, 410 nm, 355 nm, and 305 nm, respectively. Based on experimental results and theoretical calculations, they proposed a scheme of the band structure to explain the positions of the trapping states in the bandgap. The CDs exhibits five emission bands (500 nm, 445 nm, 410 nm, 355 nm, and 305 nm) corresponding to the electron transition at CO (2.5 eV), amino N (2.8 eV), pyridine N (3.0 eV), graphitic N (3.5 eV), and intrinsic C (4.1 eV) related levels, respectively [68,69].

From the above description, it is clear that there is no consistent explanation on the origin of the UV emissions from CDs yet. We believe the UV emissions in our CDs are from the anchoring PEG 200 for four reasons: 1) The PLE of the UV emission is very similar to PLE of PEG-200 as shown in Fig. 10 -left; 2) The UV emission of CDs is not the same as the first emission peak of PEG but overlaps with the second peak of PEG as displayed in Fig. 10-left. It was also reported [70] that PEG would shift emission to around 390 nm as nanoparticles are formed, which is close to the UV emission of our CDs. 3) The change of the UV emission with Na2S is about the same trend as the 310 nm emission peak with Na2S as shown in Fig. 10-right. 4) No UV emissions were observed for CDs synthesized with p-phenylenediamine without PEG 200 [35]. We also confirmed this by making CDs with p-phenylenediamine without PEG 200. We noticed that the 310 nm emission of PEG 200 is decreased gradually with Na2S and quenched completely with Na2S. However, the 375 nm emission of PEG 200 is decreased gradually and then decreased slightly at higher concentration of Na2S. The 375 nm emission peak of PEG 200 has a good overlap with the excitation peak of CDs at 455 nm. Therefore, the energy transfer from PEG to the blue center is very effective, which could be one of the reasons that the blue emission is increased with Na2S concentration.

The Blue Luminescence at 455 nm is an interesting emission that was not observed in the pristine CDs. However, when Na2S was added to the CDs, this blue emission appeared and increased gradually with the increase in Na2S concentration. This blue emission cannot be from the π-π∗ transition from the sp2 hybridization in the carbon cores because the π-π∗ transition of CDs with the average size of 0.93 nm should produce emission with wavelength peaking at around 390 nm [71]. One possibility is that the blue emission could be from p-phenylenediamine. However, p-phenylenediamine has an emission at around 400 nm rather than 455 nm (Fig. 11 ). In addition, after adding Na2S, the emission of the p-phenylenediamine decreases gradually as shown in Fig. 11. All these exclude the possibility of the blue emission from p-phenylenediamine. We did not see any luminescence from p-phenylenediamine on the CDs, indicating that p-phenylenediamine has become CDs in the hydrothermal processes and lost its properties. Obviously, the blue emission is related to Na2S and is attributed to sulfur-doped defects, as reported in the literature [62,72,73]. It was reported that the occurrence of sulfur creates extra energy levels between π and π∗ of carbon [74]. The introduction of sulfur raises the probability of electron transition both in absorption and fluorescence; therefore, the presence of the sulfur-related energy level plays a vital role in tuning the electronic structure and optical properties of the S-GQDs. More interestingly, sulfur doping was found to enhance the blue emission of CDs [74]. These phenomena support further the attribution of the blue emission at 455 nm to sulfur-related defects.

The interaction of Na2S with CDs results in the change in the luminescence of CDs, which can be used for H2S detection. We try to understand their interactions by monitoring the pH changes, H2S reduction, FITR, luminescence decays, Raman, and XPS as described below:

Influence of pH: It is clear that Na2S would help to increase pH values of CDs solution. Accordingly, we evaluated the influence of the pH on the CDs solutions and found that the luminescence is not sensitive to the pH values, as shown in Fig. 12 (a). Our result is similar to what was reported in literature [74,75]. This means the luminescence of our CDs is not sensitive to pH changes. However, changes in pH have effects on the detection of H2S. As shown in Fig. 12b, the ratios of pH = 5.0–7.0 are the highest and stable. Therefore, the pH of carbon point solution for H2S detection was 5.0 in the experiment.

The FTIR measurement shows that there are many organic species on the surface of CDs (Fig. 13 (left)). The representative functional groups are O–H (3423 cm−1/3326 cm−1), N–H (3212 cm−1), C–H (2918 cm−1/2866 cm−1), CO (1610 cm−1), CC (1503 cm−1), C–N (1297 cm−1), C–O (1103 cm−1), and NH (817 cm−1). They are from the precursors (p-phenylenediamine) and the stabilizer (PEG 200) or the solvent (ethanol). As discussed before, these organic species may contribute to the extrinsic luminescence from surface states of CDs, graphene, or graphene oxides [35,41,45]. The assignment of the green emission to the surface states can be evidenced by the chemical reduction of the CDs by NaBH4 as shown in Fig. 13 (right). When CDs were treated with NaBH4, a strong reducing agent, the emission was shifted from 523 nm to 504 nm, while the emission intensity decreased. This is a strong evidence to support the claim that the green emission is from the surface oxygenous groups, such as carbonyl and hydroxyl groups, as reported in literature [41].

It was reported that the occurrence of localized finite-sized molecular sp2 nanodomains within the sp3 matrix results in the confinement of π electrons. The intrinsic blue emission with a significant contribution from the nanodomains exhibits evidently faster decay as compared to the long decays from the extrinsic green emission, mostly corresponding to the surface states from the oxygenous groups [32,35,38,41,76]. Here, we observed similar phenomena for the green and blue emissions from our CDs, as shown in Fig. 14 . The green emission has a long decay lifetime of 29.27 ns, while blue emission has a short decay lifetime of 12.85 ns. These results further support that the green emission is from the extrinsic surface states, whereas the blue emission is from the intrinsic core states.

It is particularly interesting to observe that the decay of the green emission shortened to 14.04 ns after treating with H2S. This means the contribution from the intrinsic core state increases after interaction with S2−, likely due to surface passivation, which can be seen from the FTIR (Fig. 13 left) and the Raman spectra (Fig. 15 ), respectively. After reaction with H2S, three functional groups related to H2S appear on the CDs: S–H (2513 cm−1), CS (1195 cm−1), and C–S (703 cm−1), indicating that S2− interacted with the CDs and the oxygenous groups reduced and the surfaces were passivated. So, it is reasonable to attribute the blue emission to S-related defects in the CDs.

The interaction of S2− with CDs is further evidenced by the Raman spectra (Fig. 15). The Raman spectra of CDs show two modes of vibration at 1564 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 , which correspond to the optical mode of vibration (G-band) of two neighboring carbon atoms on a graphene layer and disordered nature as a result of doping effects (D-band), respectively [77]. After interacting with H2S, the D-band shifted toward lower wavenumber because of increased sulfur related defects, suggesting the more disordered structure involving carbon-sulfur bond. The observed intensity (ID/IG) ratio changed from CDs (0.84–0.88) after interaction with H2S is likely due to the structural distortion due to S2− interaction in the graphene lattice. Also, it indicates that the intercalation of S atoms in conjugated carbon that has more defects on the surface of CDs, may enhance the chemical and electronic properties of CDs.

The XPS spectra of CDs show the presence of only carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms. In the presence of Na2S, CDs show a visible peak corresponding to S2p. As shown in Fig. 16 , a new sulfur signal can be observed in the XPS spectra, suggesting that S species have been successfully incorporated into CDs upon the addition of Na2S. The S2p signal showed five doublets, where the binding energy values ranged between 161.8 eV and 169.1 eV. Five separate components were utilized in order to fit the SKLL signal spectra, as summarized in Table 4 .

According to the literature [78,79], alkali metal sulfides possess high reactivity and fast oxidative polymerization to generate polysulfides. Moreover, sulfur atoms in the center (central-S) and at the end (terminal-S) of the chain have different curve fitting. In our case, two peaks at 161.8 and 162.5 eV is attributed to terminal-S and central-S of the corresponding polysulfide in the polysulfide chains, respectively. The presence of a doublet separated by 0.7 eV indicated that the excess sulfides in CDs were oxidized, and polysulfide chains were present. Meanwhile, XPS spectra showed the presence of C–S–C signal for carbon-bound sulfur atoms (S2p3/2 = 163.0 eV). The S2p3/2–S2p1/2 doublet at 167.9 and 169.1 eV is ascribed to sulfate, in accordance with previous data [80]. The results indicated that chemical covalent bonding of S with CDs was formed since Na2S are very reactive.

To sum up, there are two mechanisms for H2S detection. The interactions of H2S with the CDs and the anchored ligands, as well as the energy transfer between the ligands and the CDs, are responsible for the luminescence responses for H2S detection.

Even thought the use of Cdots for H2S detection has been reported in literature [14,31,48], our observations reported here have several and unique phenomena that never reported previously: 1) We observed a striking phenomenon on the blue emission at 455 nm, which is not shown in the freshly-prepared samples, it appeared and increased linearly with the adding of Na2S; Our double ratiometric fluorescence probe for H2S monitoring based on the PEG-passivated CDs has never been reported previously; 3)Our detection is most sensitive than any Cdots based methods; and 4) Our method has the widest linear range that any H2S detection methods reported in literature.

In addition to being used in H2S detection, the carbon dots we made in such as simple way may also have many other potential applications, such as in luminescencent materials that can be used for solid state lighting [81], as light source for photodynamic activation [82,83] and as photosensitizers [84,85] for cancer treatment. Carbon dots also have been widely studied for medical applications like imaging, detecetion and therapeutics [84]. Glucose detection [86] and H2S imaging as well as heavy metal detection [68,87] would be an important area as they are closely related to our health. As new types of carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received widespread attention for their potential applications in optoelectronic device owing to their unique properties such as long hot-electron lifetime, high electron mobility, tunable bandgap, strong stable fluorescence, solution-processability, stability, non-toxicity, and low costs [81]. Applications of CDs on crops have been an emerging theme in recent years [88]. CDs at an optimal dose can promote the growth of crop plants through accelerating the photosynthesis and nutrition assimilation in plants as well as the nitrogen fixation of azotobacter. Also CDs can enhance the resistance of crops to abiotic/biotic stresses [88,89].

In summary, we investigated the luminescence of CDs as a sensor for H2S detection. The green emission of the CDs is from the extrinsic surface states, the blue emission is from the sulfur-related defects in the intrinsic core structure of CDs, while the UV emission is from the anchoring PEG 200. As Na2S is added to the CDs solution, the UV emission quenched, the green emission at 523 nm increased slightly, while a new emission appeard at 455 nm and increased gradually and linearly with the increase of Na2S concentration. Both the emission intensity ratios (F450/F350 and F455/F523) show an excellent linearity in the S2− concentration in the range of 0–800 μM, and the detection limit determined from both of them is approximately 7.0 nM, suggesting the proposed sensor is the most sensitive H2S detection sensor reported so far. Additionally, we found that our method could be used for H2S detection in live cells for practical applications and that the interactions of Na2S with the CDs and the anchored ligands, as well as the energy transfer between the ligands and the CDs, are responsible for the luminescence responses to H2S detection.

H.M. Y, L. Y. W, S. Y. W. and W. C. conceived the project, designed the experiments and wrote the manuscript. Y. X. and L. C. N. K. P and E. H. A. completed the experiments. B. B., X. D. M. S. P. D and X. H. Z. helped for data measurements and analysis. All authors discussed and commented on the paper and worked together on the paper writing.
